title: Nonlinear isotropic odd elasticity
abstract: The nonconservative elastic responses of active solids have driven a recent explosion of interest in two-dimensional "odd" elasticity: small, linear deformations of these Cauchy elastic solids enable new behaviour absent from classical, passive elasticity. Here, we establish the description of large, nonlinear deformations of isotropic two-dimensional Cauchy elastic solids. We apply our framework to the Rivlin problem, perhaps the simplest problem of elasticity lacking a linear analogue: a square deforms under dead load tractions. Surprisingly, we find that oddness suppresses the bifurcations of a passive Rivlin square. By contrast, we discover that the bifurcations of a three-dimensional Rivlin cube survive oddness even though there is no isotropic, odd linear elasticity in three dimensions. Our results thus form the basis for describing large deformations of active, biological solids while revealing their unexpected nonlinear behaviour that arises even in minimal problems.
summary: This paper develops a theory for large, nonlinear deformations in materials with 'odd' elasticity, which are non-conservative and characteristic of active biological matter. It reveals that while oddness suppresses certain symmetry-breaking transitions in 2D, these instabilities can still persist in 3D systems.
main_problem: Extending the description of 2D and 3D 'odd' elasticity from the linear, small-deformation regime to large, nonlinear deformations and investigating how non-conservative moduli affect mechanical bifurcations.
main_result: In 2D, oddness generally suppresses the bifurcation of a Rivlin square into rectangular branches, whereas in 3D, the bifurcation of a Rivlin cube survives the presence of odd elasticity.
method: The authors use continuum mechanics, polar decomposition, and power series expansions of the deformation gradient and constitutive relations to analyze stability and bifurcation conditions.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 2D and 3D isotropic Cauchy elastic solids (active/biological materials) that lack a conserved strain energy function, specifically applying the framework to the 'Rivlin problem' of a square or cube under dead load tractions.
key_observables: Principal stretches, rotation angle, Cauchy stress components, and bifurcation branches (e.g., square, rectangular, rhombic, or triaxial branches).
important_parameters: Standard elastic moduli (bulk modulus kappa and shear modulus mu) and odd elastic moduli (A and K).
main_assumptions: The materials are assumed to be isotropic and simple (local/instantaneous response); the 2D model assumes coefficients are independent of the first and second invariants.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows bifurcation diagrams for both even (passive) and odd squares, illustrating the suppression of rectangular branches and the emergence of rotated parallelogram branches. Figure 2 illustrates inhomogeneous deformation modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the nonlinear constitutive framework for 2D odd elasticity, applies it to the 2D Rivlin square problem to show bifurcation suppression, extends the analysis to 3D incompressible solids to show bifurcation survival, and concludes with the stability of inhomogeneous modes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The compressible 2D Rivlin problem. (a) Bifurcation diagram, for A = K = 0, of a square S under dead load trac- tions τ (inset): For τ < 0 (compression), the square shrinks past τ = τmin towards the fully compressed state λ = τ = 0. For τ > 0 (traction), the square expands. At τ = τ∗, the square branch bifurcates to two rectangular branches. (b) Corresponding bifurcation diagram for an odd square sat- isfying the parameter matching condition µA + κK = 0. The square rotates for τ ̸= 0. At τ = τ∗, the rotated square bifur- cates to a parallelogram.
FIG. 2. Bifurcations of a very odd compressible Rivlin square. If AK > κµ, then inhomogeneous deformations bifurcate off the square branch at τ = τ∗∗. Insets show possible modes [33].
